file_name: Shubha and Chezian - 2025 - Tripler attention model for skin lesions segmentation and detection using swin transformer networks.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Tripler Attention Model based on Swin Transformer networks and Vision Transformers (ViT) for multi-class segmentation and classification of skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.). It compares these deep learning models against CNN-based transfer learning algorithms (e.g., ResNet, DenseNet) and reports an accuracy of 92.14% for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'Section A-Research Paper' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving a proposed 'Tripler Attention Model' using Swin Transformer networks for skin lesion segmentation and detection. The paper details specific methodologies (data preprocessing, augmentation, model architecture), datasets (ISIC 2019), experimental setups (TensorFlow, GPU/CPU training), and reports original results (92.14% accuracy), distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Tripler Attention Model For Skin Lesions Segmentation And Detection Using Swin Transformer Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes a Tripler Attention Model based on Swin Transformer networks for the multi-class segmentation and detection of skin lesions, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, addressing class imbalance through data augmentation techniques such as resizing and normalization. The proposed Vision Transformer (ViT) approach was evaluated against CNN-based transfer learning algorithms, including ResNet and DenseNet variants. Performance was assessed using standard metrics like accuracy, F1 score, and AUC. The results demonstrated that the pre-trained ViT model outperformed the CNN-based algorithms, achieving an accuracy of 92.14% for skin cancer detection. This highlights the effectiveness of transformer-based architectures in handling the complexities of skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.details: The paper title and abstract explicitly mention 'Segmentation' of skin lesions. The abstract states 'Segmenting and classifying skin lesions... is known as multi-class segmentation'. The introduction mentions segmenting the affected area of skin as a first step.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on 'multi-class classification' of skin lesions into eight categories (Melanocytic Nevi, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma). The primary result reported is an accuracy of 92.14% for skin cancer detection/classification. Tables 5 and 6 provide per-class Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score for these discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermoscopy images obtained from the ISIC Archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) and hosted on Kaggle. The study notes that this dataset is unbalanced, with significant variation in sample distribution across classes.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for the experiment. The authors note it is unbalanced and applied data augmentation techniques to address class imbalance.
use.other: The paper mentions the ISIC 2019 repository as the source of the data, but Table 1 and Figure 1 explicitly label the dataset as HAM10000 with specific counts that differ from the full ISIC 2019 training set (which the text elsewhere claims has 12,875 NV images, while Table 1 lists 6,887). The study focuses on this specific subset/variant for their proposed Tripler Attention Model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compares model performance using accuracy, F1 score, AUC, and other metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The paper states that the pre-trained vision transformer outperformed CNN-based transfer learning algorithms (including ResNet, DenseNet variants) across many assessment parameters. The specific accuracy of 92.14% is reported for skin cancer detection/classification. No per-class metrics or standard deviations for the proposed model are explicitly provided in the text chunks.

architecture_used.main_model: Tripler Attention Model (Vision Transformer / ViT based)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were used; specifically, the paper mentions using the timm library to load backbones and fine-tuning pre-trained ViT models. It compares against CNN-based transfer learning algorithms (ResNet, DenseNet, VGG).

architecture_details.backbone: Vision Transformer (ViT) with Swin Transformer networks mentioned in title; utilizes multi-head self-attention blocks and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) layers with GELU activation.

training_details.framework: TensorFlow 2.7.0 (Keras API) and Python 3.8.8; also mentions PyTorch/timm in code snippet
training_details.training_time: 375.17 seconds on CPU, 82.48 seconds on GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Training samples were randomly rotated from -10 to +10 degrees. Maximal left and right rotations of up to 8 degrees were also used. Testing data sets were augmented with smaller random rotation angles.
Data was shifted horizontally and vertically by 0.2 (20% of image dimensions).
Images were zoomed-in and zoomed-out by 20%.
Empty areas created by transformations were filled with the nearest pixels.
Methods considered included adjusting brightness, saturation, hue, and contrast to simulate real-world environmental variance and lighting situations.
Included sub-techniques such as cropping, affine transformation, padding, scaling, flipping, and translation.

Pixel values were scaled between 0 and 1. Standard deviation of [0.224, 0.225, 0.229] and mean of [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] were applied to align pixel values with pretrained SWIN transformer model distributions.

conclusions: The proposed Tripler Attention Model based on pre-trained Vision Transformers (ViT) outperformed CNN-based transfer learning algorithms (including ResNet, DenseNet, and VGG variants) for skin cancer detection across multiple assessment parameters.
The model achieved an accuracy of 92.14% for skin cancer detection/classification on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
Data augmentation techniques were effective in addressing the class imbalance problem inherent in the skin lesion dataset.
The use of PolyLoss as the loss function contributed to enhancing the classification algorithm's accuracy compared to standard cross-entropy loss.
The proposed ViT-based model demonstrated resilience and effectiveness in multi-class skin cancer classification, performing admirably against state-of-the-art models.

The study highlights the potential of transformer-based architectures as a viable alternative to CNNs for automating skin cancer diagnosis, which is critical for early detection and reducing healthcare burdens.
important_remarks: The paper claims to use the ISIC 2019 dataset but explicitly uses a subset of the HAM10000 dataset (13,319 images) for the primary experiment, creating a discrepancy between the stated dataset name and the actual data used.

There is a significant internal inconsistency regarding dataset composition: Table 1 lists 7 classes from HAM10000, while Table 2 lists 8 classes from ISIC 2019; the text also references different total counts (25,331 vs 25,651) for the ISIC 2019 training set.
The study relies on a single public dataset (HAM10000/ISIC 2019) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the 92.14% accuracy claim.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed Tripler Attention Model over the baseline CNN models.
The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., 6,887 Melanocytic Nevi vs. 122 Dermatofibroma in the used subset), which may bias the model's performance metrics.

The paper does not report per-class performance metrics (precision, recall, F1) for the proposed Tripler Attention Model, only an aggregate accuracy of 92.14%, making it difficult to assess performance on minority classes.

The hardware environment for training is reported as 'Jupyter laptop' and 'Kaggle laptop', which may not reflect the computational requirements for clinical deployment or reproducibility on standard high-performance computing infrastructure.

The authors acknowledge the risk of overfitting due to the unbalanced nature of the dataset but do not provide evidence (such as learning curves or validation loss analysis) that overfitting was effectively mitigated beyond the use of data augmentation.

dataset_usage_details.source_pdf_path: /home/agarciag/Desktop/skin_cancer_review/llm_agent/pdfs/compliant/processed_Shubha and Chezian - 2025 - Tripler attention model for skin lesions segmentation and detection using swin transformer networks.pdf
dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (sourced from Kaggle/ISIC Archive) for the primary experiment. The dataset was split into a training set (5,690 images) and a test set (1,797 images), representing a 75/25 split of a specific subset of the data. The authors also reference the full ISIC 2019 training dataset (25,651 images) in Table 2, but the experimental results are based on the smaller HAM10000 subset. No external validation or cross-dataset testing was performed; the model was trained and tested on the same dataset source.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report any cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset (a subset of ISIC data). While the paper mentions ISIC 2019 in Table 2, it does not perform experiments where the model is trained on one dataset and tested on a different, unseen dataset.

training.notes: Used for training the Tripler Attention Model and baseline CNNs. The subset was derived from the larger ISIC/HAM10000 repository.

validation.notes: The paper mentions a 'Validation Set' in Figure 2 (flowchart) but does not explicitly define a separate validation split with counts for model selection. The 25% split is described as the 'test dataset', implying the remaining 75% was training. Internal validation via cross-validation or a held-out validation set is not clearly detailed with counts.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. This represents 25% of the subset used in the experiment.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet were used to initialize the Swin Transformer and CNN models (ResNet, DenseNet, etc.) before fine-tuning on the skin lesion dataset.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques (rotation, translation, zooming, padding, color augmentation, normalization) were applied to the training data to address class imbalance and increase sample size. No synthetic data generation (e.g., GANs) was used by the authors themselves, though the literature review mentions other works using GANs.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: A subset of the HAM10000 dataset was used. The text states: 'A subset of the whole dataset—25%—was used to create the test dataset, whereas the training dataset itself has 5,690 pictures.' This implies a 75/25 split of the subset. Total images in subset = 5690 + 1797 = 7487. The paper notes this subset was built by taking 1500 images from four classes (NV, BCC, Mel, BKL) and all images from the other four classes (totaling 7487).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set was drawn from the same source (HAM10000/ISIC) as the training set.

dataset_level_results.notes: The paper reports an accuracy of 92.14% for the proposed Tripler Attention Model on the test set. It claims this outperforms CNN-based transfer learning algorithms but does not provide specific comparative metrics for the baselines on this specific split in the provided text chunks.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: 75% training / 25% testing split of a 7,487 image subset

generalizability_notes: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000/ISIC) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the results.
The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., 6,887 Melanocytic Nevi vs. 122 Dermatofibroma in the full HAM10000 distribution, though the subset used has different proportions).

The paper acknowledges the risk of overfitting due to the unbalanced nature of the dataset but does not provide evidence (such as learning curves or validation loss analysis) that overfitting was effectively mitigated beyond the use of data augmentation.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed.
information_not_reported: Exact counts for the validation set if distinct from the test set (the flowchart shows a validation set, but the text only details training and test splits).
Per-class metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, AUC) for the proposed Tripler Attention Model on the test set.
Specific comparative metrics for the baseline CNN models on the same test split.
Patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient appear in both train and test sets).
